card.question: Which statement is logically equivalent to P ⇒ Q?
distractors: Q ⇒ P (the converse)
¬P ⇒ ¬Q (the inverse)
P ∧ ¬Q

card.question: The negation of  ∀x ∃y, P(x,y)  is:
distractors: ∀x ∃y, ¬P(x,y)
∃x ∃y, ¬P(x,y)
∀x ∀y, ¬P(x,y)

card.question: In a direct proof that 'n even ⇒ n² even', you start by writing:
distractors: Assume n² is odd
Suppose n is odd, so n = 2k + 1
Assume √2 = a/b

card.question: To prove 'n² even ⇒ n even' by contrapositive, you instead prove:
distractors: n even ⇒ n² even
n² odd ⇒ n odd
Assume n² even and n odd, then derive a contradiction

card.question: A proof by contradiction that √2 is irrational begins by assuming:
distractors: √2 is irrational
a and b are both even
√2 = a/b where a and b share a common factor

card.question: The inductive step of a proof by induction establishes:
card.answer: If the statement holds for n, then it holds for n + 1
distractors: The statement holds for the base case n = 1
The statement holds for all n at once
The statement fails for some n

card.question: The standard way to prove two sets A = B is to:
card.answer: Show A ⊆ B and B ⊆ A (double inclusion)
distractors: Show A and B have the same number of elements
Exhibit a bijection between A and B
Show A ∩ B = A

card.question: To prove a function f is injective, you assume:
card.answer: f(a) = f(b) and derive a = b
distractors: For every y there exists x with f(x) = y
a = b and derive f(a) = f(b)
f has an inverse, so it must be injective
